profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The problem is choosing rational regimes of traffic light control for both traffic and pedestrian flows at signalized intersections. Existing traffic light control systems operate with limited time parameters and do not adequately consider pedestrian behavior, leading to increased traffic rule violations and reduced safety at grade-level pedestrian crosswalks.
profiles.method_summary: The study employs experimental field measurements of traffic and pedestrian flow indicators at signalized intersections in Lviv city, combined with simulation modeling using PTV Vissim software. Pedestrian behavior was analyzed based on surveillance camera recordings from the city's traffic management center. The research developed quadratic equations modeling the relationship between pedestrian violations and volume-capacity ratio, and determined maximal vehicle queue lengths under different control regimes.
datasets: field_measurements_from_8_signalized_intersections_and_pedestrian_crosswalks_in_lviv_city_divided_into_3_types
surveillance_camera_data_from_lviv_city_traffic_management_center
ptv_vissim_simulation_data
matlab_computed_dependencies

main_claims.claim_text: Intersections should be classified into three types based on location characteristics and traffic/pedestrian flow patterns: Type I near transport junctions, Type II in central zones, and Type III in residential zones, each requiring different traffic light control parameters.

The smallest number of pedestrian violations occurs with restrictive signal duration of 40-60 seconds at volume-capacity ratios of 0.50-0.65 for Type I, 0.40-0.65 for Type II, and 0.45-0.55 for Type III intersections.

The maximum vehicle queue length varies significantly by intersection type, reaching 504.56 m for Type I at volume-capacity ratio 0.88, compared to 353.65 m for Type II and 257.88 m for Type III under similar conditions.

Rational traffic light control should limit restrictive signal duration to maximum 50 seconds for Type I, 90 seconds for Type II, and 110 seconds for Type III intersections, regardless of control phase number.

limitations.text: The study was conducted only in Lviv city, which may limit generalizability to other cities with different traffic patterns, infrastructure, or cultural contexts.
Lane width was found to have no impact on pedestrian behavior but the study focused only on widths between 3.0-4.0 meters.
The model coefficients for violation prediction had R2 values ranging from 0.70 to 0.89, indicating moderate fit accuracy.
Climate conditions and pedestrian discipline factors were identified as important but not fully incorporated into the developed models.

future_work: Further research could extend the methodology to other cities, incorporate real-time adaptive traffic control systems, develop dynamic models accounting for weather conditions and seasonal variations, and explore additional intersection types beyond the three categories studied.
profiles.notes: The study provides practical recommendations for traffic engineers to optimize signal timing based on intersection location type and volume-capacity ratio. The methodology combining field observations with PTV Vissim simulation offers a replicable approach for other urban settings. Key finding is that longer pedestrian waiting times paradoxically increase violations due to group behavior dynamics.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing decision-making at uncontrolled intersections with continuous traffic flow, and developing computational models to characterize pedestrian behavior based on behavioral mechanisms. The study addresses gaps in existing pedestrian behavior models which lack psychological underpinnings, don't adequately model traffic flow scenarios, and fail to capture the time-dynamic nature of crossing decisions.
profiles.method_summary: A deconstructed crossing decision-making process is proposed with three stages: visual collision cue, decision, and crossing initiation. The visual collision cue uses the first temporal derivative of visual angle (˙θ) as the primary decision cue. A binary choice logit model with decision strategies for traffic flow scenarios models gap acceptance. Crossing initiation time is modeled using Shifted Wald (SW) distribution. Two datasets from a CAVE-based immersive pedestrian simulator calibrate and validate the models.
datasets: Dataset one: 60 participants, 4270 trials, single-lane road with two consecutive vehicles at speeds 25/30/35 mph and time gaps 2/3/4/5 s (CAVE simulator)
Dataset two: 60 participants, 1920 trials, continuous traffic flow with varying gap sequences (1-8s) across 4 traffic scenarios (CAVE simulator)
baselines: G-PRD model (Gaussian distribution-based Pedestrian Road-crossing Decision model) - compared in validation

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing decisions can be characterized as dynamically adapting based on perceived collision risk, where the visual collision cue (˙θ, first temporal derivative of visual angle) effectively captures pedestrian gap acceptance behavior across different traffic scenarios

The SW-PRD model (Shifted Wald-based Pedestrian Road-crossing Decision model) significantly outperforms the G-PRD model on both datasets, achieving better BIC values, higher log-likelihoods, and passing K-S tests while G-PRD fails

The proposed decision strategy for continuous traffic flow (comparing collision cues of current gap with maximum rejected gap and following gap) significantly improves model performance in traffic flow scenarios

Pedestrians dynamically adjust their crossing initiation time based on vehicle kinematics - initiating faster when facing higher collision threat - demonstrating the time-dynamic nature of crossing decisions

The model reproduces pedestrian crossing behavior patterns in continuous traffic flow, including reduced tendency to accept gaps when upcoming larger gaps exist and greater tendency to reject gaps after previously rejecting similar or larger gaps

limitations.text: Model only covers scenarios with single-lane, constant-speed traffic and cannot be directly generalized to multi-lane scenarios, yielding vehicles, or complex traffic conditions without further development
Experiments conducted in pedestrian simulator may not fully capture real-world behavior variability - virtual nature of task may affect observed behavior compared to naturalistic settings
Model assumes pedestrians use specific visual cues (˙θ) and perception strategies based on current collision perception theory, but actual pedestrian cognitive processes may differ
Model does not account for external factors such as weather conditions, visibility, pedestrian demographics, or social influences that may affect real-world crossing behavior

future_work: Apply model to reliable naturalistic datasets to validate in real-world traffic
Extend model to multi-lane scenarios with refuges
Incorporate yielding vehicle behavior and more complex traffic dynamics
Investigate effects of waiting time separately from traffic flow patterns
Integrate model with AV planning and control systems for pedestrian intention prediction
Use model as module in microscopic transport simulation tools or virtual testing platforms for AV validation
profiles.notes: The paper introduces anthropomorphic modeling approach to pedestrian crossing decisions by grounding the model in visual perception theory. The deconstructed three-stage process (perceive-decide-execute) provides framework for understanding pedestrian behavior. The study provides a simulation tool for reproducing pedestrian crossing behavior. Model shows good predictive performance with R²=0.890 for gap acceptance and R²=0.850 for crossing initiation time on Dataset one.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: How to quantitatively evaluate the impact of pedestrian policies (specifically lane-like segregation) on pedestrian behavior before implementation, using microscopic simulation
profiles.method_summary: A microscopic pedestrian simulation model (MPSM) based on physical forces (forward force and two types of repulsive forces) using discrete event simulation with difference equations. The model treats pedestrians as autonomous circular objects with individual properties (initial location, time, velocity, target). Unlike similar physical-based models (social force, magnetic force), this model does not require target time as input and outputs dissipation time. Parameters include calibrated values: max speed 1.2 m/s, pedestrian diameter 70 cm, sight distance 4 m, α=15, β=6, mass=2.
datasets: calibration_using_real_pedestrian_behavior

main_claims.claim_text: The microscopic pedestrian simulation model based on physical forces can effectively evaluate pedestrian policies quantitatively before implementation
Lane-like segregation policy produces higher average walking speed than mix-lane policy, with speed difference increasing with pedestrian density
Lane-like segregation policy results in significantly lower delay compared to mix-lane policy, with delay changes remaining low across pedestrian numbers
Lane-like segregation policy reduces dissipation time compared to mix-lane policy, enabling faster crossing completion for the same number of pedestrians

limitations.text: The simulation model is merely a numerical experiment rather than a complete real world simulation
Model lacks validation against real-world pedestrian behavior data; validation is recommended for further study

future_work: Validation of the simulation model toward real world data
Further calibration and testing with empirical pedestrian flow data
profiles.notes: Model differs from social force and magnetic force models by not requiring target time as input; Uses discrete event simulation without queuing theory; Case study: pedestrian crossing at Tohoku University; Policy implementation involves marking arrows on left side of zebra cross to guide pedestrians

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Traffic signal scheduling in urban networks that considers both pedestrians and vehicles, addressing the imbalance where existing studies focus mainly on vehicles leading to substantial pedestrian delays
profiles.method_summary: Novel pedestrian hopping model with logic constraints describing pedestrian flow dynamics; formulated as MILP for delay minimization and solved using both exact method (GUROBI) and meta-heuristic (Discrete Harmony Search algorithm); integrated pedestrian-vehicle scheduling using weighted sum approach
method_family: Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) + Meta-heuristic (Discrete Harmony Search)
datasets: Synthetic simulation data - networks from 3x3 to 10x10 junctions with varying prediction horizons (15-90 seconds)
baselines: Pure vehicle model vs pure pedestrian model
Pedestrian delay model vs pedestrian unhappiness model

main_claims.claim_text: The pedestrian hopping model effectively captures pedestrian flow dynamics in urban networks by describing capacity changes between waiting zones through a hopping rule
MILP can solve pedestrian traffic scheduling for 10x10 junctions with 30-second horizon in 1.4 seconds (9.4% of sampling period), making real-time implementation feasible
DHS algorithm provides near-optimal solutions with less than 20% gap for large networks while reducing computation time from 59.96s (MILP) to 0.6s for 10x10 network with 90s horizon
The pedestrian unhappiness model significantly reduces the phenomenon where few pedestrians wait for long time by providing fairness through exponential function evaluation
Pedestrian impact on vehicle delays can be captured as a piecewise constant function, showing vehicle network is insensitive to pedestrian changes within certain weight ranges

limitations.text: Pedestrian demand, diversion ratios, and crosswalk capacities are assumed known in advance rather than measured in real-time
Only considers conventional two-way crossing; exclusive pedestrian phases (diagonal crossing) not considered
Pedestrian non-compliance behavior is not modeled - assumes pedestrians stop at Flashing GREEN
Case study only considers one-way vehicle flows; more complex phases with turning flows not addressed
Real-time scheduling cannot be guaranteed for large urban networks using MILP alone due to computational complexity

future_work: Relax assumptions by using real-time data-driven model identification
Extend integrated model to include complex vehicle turning flows
Address larger networks with guaranteed real-time performance
Further validate with real-world pedestrian network data
profiles.notes: ArXiv preprint (1705.05251); combines macroscopic pedestrian flow model with vehicle Cell Transmission Model; uses GUROBI optimizer; proposes both linear (delay) and nonlinear (unhappiness) optimization objectives

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating the impact of smartphone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and evaluating the effectiveness of a smart LED light safety treatment using head-mounted immersive virtual reality
profiles.method_summary: A Virtual Immersive/Interactive Reality Environment (VIRE) with Oculus Rift was developed to simulate an unsignalized road crossing in Montreal. Forty-two participants completed 30 successful crossing trials across three conditions: 1) no distraction, 2) distracted with smartphone maze-solving game, and 3) distracted with smartphone plus smart LED safety measure. Data on surrogate safety measures (PET, TTC), crossing behavior, and distraction attributes were collected and analyzed using Multinomial Logit (MNL) models.
datasets: 42 volunteers from Montreal and Toronto (59.9% Montreal, 40.5% female, ages 18-45)
30 successful crossing trials per participant (10 per condition)
Total of approximately 1260 trials analyzed
metrics: Percentage of time looking at smartphone
Number of head orientations to smartphone

main_claims.claim_text: Distracted pedestrians have significantly lower PET measures and higher crossing speeds, resulting in more dangerous crossings compared to non-distracted pedestrians

The smart LED flashing and color-changing lights installed on crosswalks improve pedestrian safety by increasing PET measures by 13% and improving the successful crossing rate by 7.4% compared to the untreated distracted condition
Females exhibit more dangerous crossing behavior than males, especially under distracted conditions; however, the smart LED treatment reduces this negative impact

The number of head orientations toward the smartphone during a trial and the percentage of time looking at the smartphone during waiting time significantly increase the probability of unsafe crossings

limitations.text: Single road crossing location in Montreal limits generalizability to other urban environments
Study only examined interactions between vehicles and pedestrians; mixed traffic scenarios with cyclists were not included
Eye tracking was not used; head orientation was used as proxy for gaze direction
Only maze-solving puzzle was tested as distraction; other distraction types (texting, calling, listening to music) were not examined
Sample size of 42 participants may limit statistical power for detecting smaller effect sizes
VR environment despite high fidelity may not fully replicate real-world pedestrian behavior due to absence of actual physical risk

future_work: Incorporate mixed traffic scenarios with vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists
Add eye-tracking technology for more precise distraction measurement
Compare different distraction types (calling, texting, listening to music) on crossing behavior
Test different safety treatment designs and implementations
Evaluate the approach across multiple geographic locations and crossing types
Study long-term behavioral changes and habituation effects
profiles.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; Accepted June 17, 2018; VIRE system replicates real Montreal urban intersection near Laurier metro station; Smart LED lights change from white to blue when distracted crossing is initiated; Participants were not informed of success/failure during trials; Average walking speed of 1.1 m/s consistent with field studies; 62% of participants had never used VR before

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Current pedestrian behavior models for traffic simulation are limited in utility for intelligent driving applications due to situation-specific implementations, rigid environment definitions, limited pedestrian characteristics, simplistic gap estimation for crossing decisions, and rigid decision-making steps. There is a need for highly configurable simulations that can generate diverse pedestrian populations with realistic crossing behaviors.
profiles.domain: Intelligent Transportation Systems / Autonomous Driving / Pedestrian Behavior Modeling
profiles.method_summary: The paper proposes Intend-Wait-Cross, an agent-based microscopic pedestrian behavior model that generates realistic crossing decisions. The model incorporates: (1) six individual characteristics: type (adult/child/elderly), trait (aggressive/conservative/average), law obedience, gap acceptance, crossing pattern (one-stage/rolling gap), and perceptual noise; (2) a discrete choice model for multiple crossing scenarios; (3) an improved TTC computation accounting for vehicle relevance (positional and intentional), dynamic vehicle behavior, pedestrian travel time, vehicle length, and perceptual noise. The model is implemented in SUMO traffic simulation platform.
method_family: Agent-based modeling / Microscopic traffic simulation / Discrete choice model
datasets: PIE (Pedestrian Intention Estimation) dataset - naturalistic driving data
Knoblauch et al. walking speed field study
Schmidt et al. gap acceptance study (Europe)
Ashworth gap acceptance study (Europe)
Brewer et al. gap acceptance study (North America)
tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision-making at designated crosswalks and jaywalking scenarios
Traffic simulation for autonomous driving applications
metrics: Minimum TTC at time of crossing
baselines: Average TTC method (average of current and max speed)
Dynamic TTC method (accounting for vehicle acceleration)
Dynamic+adj TTC (with pedestrian travel time and vehicle length adjustment)

main_claims.claim_text: The Intend-Wait-Cross model generates diverse pedestrian behaviors by incorporating individual characteristics (type, trait, law obedience, gap acceptance, crossing pattern, perceptual noise) that are motivated by traffic behavior research.

The dynamic TTC computation with adjustment for pedestrian travel time and vehicle length (Dynamic+adj) nearly eliminates vehicle-pedestrian collisions (0.0-0.4 collisions) across light, medium, and heavy traffic conditions, compared to 3.2-7.0 collisions with constant and average methods.

Law obedience and pedestrian traits significantly affect crossing behavior: average pedestrians show 4-6% illegal crossings matching PIE dataset statistics, while aggressive pedestrians accept shorter gaps and have higher jaywalking rates due to faster walking speeds and lower gap acceptance thresholds.

Rolling gap crossing pattern results in significantly shorter wait times than one-stage crossing across all traffic conditions, while one-stage crossing leads to longer crossing durations in light and medium traffic due to pedestrians waiting for all lanes to clear.

limitations.text: The vehicle reactive behavior model has limitations - vehicles only detect pedestrians when paths are aligned, causing remaining accidents when pedestrians cross very close to intersections and vehicles are about to turn
The model parameters for trait, law-obedience, and crosswalk distance threshold were set empirically rather than derived from naturalistic data due to lack of available data
Pedestrian gap acceptance values were set based on European studies which may not generalize to all countries and environments

future_work: Extend the vehicle behavior model to improve detection of pedestrians near intersections
Implement pedestrian group dynamics
Improve perception models for all agents
profiles.notes: Published on arXiv (2203.07324v1) in March 2022. Implementation uses SUMO (v1.9.0) traffic simulation platform. Model is motivated by traffic behavior literature and calibrated using naturalistic driving data (PIE dataset) and estimates from the literature. The paper addresses the need for realistic pedestrian simulations for training and evaluating autonomous driving systems.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses the lack of pedestrian experience consideration in intelligent transportation systems (ITS). Traffic signals traditionally focus on vehicle throughput and waiting time while overlooking pedestrians. There is no accurate simulation framework for estimating pedestrian crossing time at signalized intersections, which is needed for dynamic traffic light scheduling that balances pedestrian safety and convenience with vehicular flow.
profiles.domain: Intelligent Transportation Systems, Traffic Management, Pedestrian Simulation
profiles.method_summary: The paper presents a novel Pedestrian Road Crossing Simulator (PCS) that models pedestrian motion at signalized intersections at a fine-grained level. PCS uses a geometric approach where each pedestrian is represented by a circle representing occupied space that cannot overlap with others. The simulator employs Normal distribution for pedestrian initial positioning in waiting areas, models different pedestrian types (healthy adults, elders, children, disabled persons) with varying speeds, and uses exclusion arc calculations to determine optimal movement paths. The system is validated using field data collected from two intersections in Xi'an, China, and integrated with SUMO traffic simulation for TLS optimization experiments.
datasets: Field data from intersection of Ming Guang Road and Feng Cheng No. 9 Road, Xi'an (Location1) - crosswalk dimensions 47.69m x 6.4m (North/South), 43.62m x 3.6m (West/East)
Field data from intersection of Wei Yang Road and Feng Cheng No. 7 Road, Xi'an (Location2) - crosswalk dimensions 45.045m x 3.6m
Pedestrian walking speed distribution: N(1.2676, 0.09167)
Video recordings from mobile phones documenting pedestrian and vehicle counts, directions, and crossing times
Synthetic traffic data: 990 vehicles and 522 pedestrians
tasks: Pedestrian crossing time estimation at signalized intersections
Dynamic traffic light scheduling optimization incorporating pedestrian needs
Pedestrian behavior simulation including group dynamics and mobility constraints
metrics: Estimation accuracy of crossing time (98.37% claimed)
baselines: Static TLS with fixed phases and duration (matching Figure 14: 84s, 3s, 50s, 3s per phase)
Dynamic TLS adaptive to vehicle traffic demand only (without pedestrian consideration)

main_claims.claim_text: PCS achieves 98.37% accuracy in estimating pedestrian crossing time compared to real field measurements
Integrating PCS into dynamic TLS reduces pedestrian average waiting time by 77.44% compared to static TLS while maintaining high vehicular throughput
Dynamic TLS with PCS reduces vehicle average waiting time by 66.02% compared to static TLS
PCS handles complex scenarios including pedestrian groups crossing from both directions and various pedestrian types with different mobility constraints

future_work: Extend PCS to handle multiple adjacent intersections for coordinated pedestrian-vehicle flow optimization
Develop algorithms to improve throughput of both vehicles and pedestrians across multiple intersections
Study pedestrian behavior correlation across multiple adjacent intersections
Incorporate more diverse pedestrian behavior models beyond Normal distribution
profiles.notes: PCS is made available as open source at github.com/DayuanTan/OpenSourceAcademicResearch; The simulator uses SUMO v1.9.2 for traffic simulation and Python 3.7.2 for implementation; Field study collected data at two different times (2pm and 7pm) to capture different traffic densities; Three distributions tested for pedestrian positioning: Normal, Poisson, and T distributions; Buffer zones around crosswalks were introduced based on field observations of actual pedestrian behavior

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Understanding collective traffic phenomena of molecular motors in cells, including traffic jam formation, boundary-induced phase transitions, and spontaneous symmetry breaking in multi-species motor systems
profiles.method_summary: Uses driven lattice gas models (variants of asymmetric exclusion processes) to describe molecular motor traffic. Models incorporate active movement of bound motors as biased random walks, passive diffusion of unbound motors, motor-filament binding/unbinding dynamics, and motor-motor mutual exclusion interactions. Three-dimensional cubic lattice with filament sites and non-filament sites, with rates for stepping, unbinding, rebinding, and exclusion.

main_claims.claim_text: For filaments in contact with a constant motor reservoir (large unbound motor pool), the traffic jam length is of the order of the motor walking distance Δxb = vb/ε0

In confined geometries such as tube-like compartments, traffic jams can become much longer than the walking distance and scale as L* ~ 1/vb rather than L* ~ Δxb, withjam length diverging logarithmically as the boundary density approaches unity

Spontaneous symmetry breaking occurs in systems with two motor species moving in opposite directions when motor-motor interactions exceed a critical threshold qc, leading to one species occupying the filament while the other is excluded, independent of boundary conditions

limitations.text: Analysis assumes unbound motor density gradients along the filament direction can be neglected, which may not hold in all experimental situations
Theoretical predictions have limited experimental validation; only a few observations of motor traffic jams exist in vivo and in vitro
Model parameters (binding/unbinding rates, diffusion coefficients) must be carefully matched to specific motor types (kinesin, dynein, myosin) for quantitative predictions

future_work: The paper mentions studying two-species systems leading to symmetry breaking and traffic lane formation, but does not explicitly discuss future research directions
profiles.notes: This paper provides a theoretical framework connecting molecular motor biology with statistical physics of driven diffusive systems. Key insight: unlike macroscopic traffic, molecular motors have finite run lengths and can unbind/rebind, leading to unique collective phenomena. The work bridges biophysical experiments with nonequilibrium statistical mechanics.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Developing a stochastic cellular automaton model for pedestrian dynamics that can quantitatively reproduce the fundamental diagram and self-organizing behaviors while compensating for deficiencies caused by the discrete grid structure
profiles.method_summary: A microscopic stochastic cellular automaton model using a 3x3 transition matrix (Moore neighborhood) for agent movements. The model includes grid-based path planning via flood fill algorithm with Moore neighborhood distance metric, compensation strategies for grid-induced directional dependencies, and a dynamic motion field for mid-range agent interactions that stores both past positions and extrapolated future positions. The model uses shuffled sequential update procedure and motion traces.
datasets: infinite_environment_simulation

main_claims.claim_text: The stochastic cellular automaton model successfully reproduces the characteristic shape of the fundamental diagram of pedestrian dynamics with vmax = 4 (3) speed characteristic
The model reproduces self-organizing behaviors including lane formation in counter-flow scenarios through the dynamic motion field component
Compensation settings for the transition matrix successfully address directional dependencies caused by the underlying regular grid structure

The Moore metric flood fill algorithm exactly reproduces shortest paths equivalent to Euclidean distance while preventing directional artifacts through combination of clockwise and counter-clockwise vector fields
The stochastic approach adequately handles uncertainties in human motion patterns without requiring artificial noise terms

limitations.text: The regular grid structure causes inherent directional dependencies affecting motion speed and variance, particularly at diagonal transitions
The stochastic cell selection process introduces additional motion variance for non-axis transition angles
Lane formations are not stable in time and space due to the inherent stochastic nature of the model
The Moore metric tends to overestimate distances compared to Euclidean metric

future_work: The paper mentions that detailed parameterization of the dynamic motion field needs to be done as proof of concept only
profiles.notes: Model uses 3-4 motion steps per simulation turn depending on agent density; The dynamic motion field combines past positions and future positions with isotropic spatial diffusion and temporal decay; The algorithm does not require computation-time expensive sorting or square root calculations; Counter-flow scenario demonstrates separation of agents and lane formation

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating the impact of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems on traffic dynamics, specifically whether automated driving strategies can improve road capacity and traffic stability or will necessarily increase heterogeneity and instability.
profiles.method_summary: Microscopic traffic simulations using the Intelligent Driver Model (IDM) to study mixed traffic consisting of human-driven and ACC-equipped vehicles on a single-lane road with an on-ramp bottleneck. Three ACC parameter sets were tested varying time headway (λT), acceleration (λa), and deceleration (λb).
datasets: synthetic_simulation_data_generated_from_idm

main_claims.claim_text: A small proportion of ACC vehicles (as low as 10%) leads to a drastic reduction of traffic congestion, with maximum travel time delay reduced by about 30% and cumulated time delay reduced by 50%
ACC vehicles increase both free capacity and dynamic capacity of traffic flow, with Q_free_max increasing approximately linearly with ACC percentage
The time headway parameter T is the most crucial factor for capacity improvement, with the relative gain formula γ ≈ [0.95(1-λT) + 0.07λa + 0.08(1-λb)]αACC showing λT has the highest coefficient
Traffic breakdown can be completely prevented with approximately 30% ACC vehicles using jam-avoiding driving strategies

ACC systems described by car-following models can stabilize traffic better than human drivers due to negligible response time, though human drivers achieve stability through spatial and temporal anticipation

limitations.text: Single-lane scenario does not account for multi-lane effects that are relevant in real traffic
Trucks are excluded from simulations, which may affect real-world applicability
Merging, lane changing and gap-creation behaviors are not modeled (still require human driver intervention)
Results are based on synthetic simulation data rather than empirical validation with real ACC-equipped vehicles

future_work: Implement an intelligent ACC strategy that dynamically adapts the driving style to the overall traffic situation
Use inter-vehicle communication to contribute information about downstream traffic conditions
Design ACC systems that consider system-level impact on traffic dynamics rather than only individual comfort and safety
Explore detection of traffic states using vehicle sensor data in combination with digital maps
profiles.notes: Paper published in 2006 on arXiv (physics.soc-ph). Uses Intelligent Driver Model (IDM) for both human and ACC driving, differentiating only by parameter sets. Key finding is that small percentages of ACC can have disproportionate positive effects on traffic flow due to capacity improvements and stabilization.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Predicting pedestrian crossing behavior including crossing decision (cross or wait), crossing initiation time (CIT), and crossing duration (CD) when interacting with vehicles at unsignalized crossings
profiles.problem_type: Classification and regression (binary classification for crossing decision, regression for CIT and CD)
profiles.domain: Automated driving, pedestrian-vehicle interaction, intelligent transportation systems
profiles.method_summary: Machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, neural network MLP) to predict pedestrian crossing behavior using features including time to arrival (TTA), waiting time, crossing location type, age, gender, SVO (social value orientation), and AISS (Arnett inventory of sensation seeking). Uses distributed simulator data with 64 participant pairs and 1279 trials.
method_family: Machine learning - Logistic Regression, Support-Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network
datasets: Distributed Simulator Study (DSS) - University of Leeds Driving Simulator (UoLDS) connected to HIKER pedestrian lab
64 participant pairs (32 drivers, 32 pedestrians)
1279 total trials, 836 crossing cases
Five TTA conditions (3, 4, 5, 6, 7 seconds)
Four crossing locations (two zebra, two non-zebra)
tasks: Crossing decision prediction (cross or wait - binary classification)
metrics: Accuracy (ACC) and F1 score for classification
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for regression
baselines: Logistic regression baseline from Kalantari et al. [5]
Linear regression baseline for CIT and CD from Kalantari et al. [5]

main_claims.claim_text: The proposed MLP model improves crossing decision prediction accuracy by 4.46% and F1 score by 3.23% compared to logistic regression baseline
The RF model reduces CIT prediction RMSE by 21.56% compared to linear regression baseline
The MLP model reduces CD prediction RMSE by 30.14% compared to linear regression baseline
Non-linear models (RF, MLP) significantly outperform linear models for non-zebra crossing prediction, improving accuracy by 8.75% and F1 by 10.82%
Personality traits (AISS, SVO) contribute to crossing decision prediction in a non-linear way, with non-linear models relying more on these features than linear models

limitations.text: The study uses simulator data which may not fully represent real-world pedestrian behavior
In practice, it is difficult to obtain all features (personality traits, age, gender) used in the models, limiting real-world applicability
Performance degrades significantly when using fewer input features, particularly for non-linear models
Smaller TTA values lead to lower prediction accuracy due to increased uncertainty in pedestrian behavior

future_work: Include pedestrians' trajectories and postures to support building predictive models
Extend model to naturalistic data for broader validation
Investigate real-time prediction capabilities
Explore additional personality and contextual factors
profiles.notes: Paper was accepted for publication in 2023 IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV). Funded by European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (SHAPE-IT project, grant agreement 860410). The study is novel in considering both objective factors and subjective personality traits (SVO, AISS) for pedestrian-vehicle interaction prediction at unsignalized crossings.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: The paper addresses limitations in current pedestrian dynamics models, which primarily focus on operational movement aspects (spatial exclusion, distance and direction repulsion) while neglecting psychological influences and group coordination. The research investigates group dynamic behavior and psychometric profiles as drivers for pedestrian dynamics, aiming to incorporate psychophysical concepts and intra-group coordination into movement models.
profiles.problem_type: Empirical research with observational study and experimental components
profiles.domain: Pedestrian dynamics / Traffic and Transportation Science / Interdisciplinary (traffic sciences, sociology, mathematics, physics, psychology)
profiles.method_summary: The study uses mixed methods: (1) Field observations at German Protestant Kirchentag mass event (120,000 participants) to analyze group behavior and density patterns; (2) Evacuation experiment at TU Dresden with 301 students, combining video tracking and psychometric questionnaires (EPQ-RK); (3) Statistical analysis including t-tests, ANOVAs, and Spearman correlations to examine personality effects on movement decisions and walking patterns.
method_family: Empirical observational study / Mixed methods (quantitative movement tracking + psychometric questionnaires)
datasets: German Protestant Kirchentag at Dresden (June 1-5, 2011) - approximately 120,000 fulltime participants and 50,000 guests
Evacuation experiment at TU Dresden - 301 students participated, 259 completed questionnaires, 188 matched with movement data, 88 with complete personality data
tasks: Group detection and tracking from video data
Personality trait measurement (extraversion, neuroticism, psychoticism via EPQ-RK)
Analysis of pre-movement phase parameters (tready, tstart)
Analysis of movement phase parameters (walking speed, speed deviation, detour index)
metrics: Probability density function (PDF) for relative positions
Z-standardized time deviations from group mean
baselines: Moussaïd et al. (2010) group behavior study

main_claims.claim_text: Group size significantly affects pedestrian speed characteristics - median and standard deviation decrease with group size up to 5 members, then show an unexpected increase at larger groups

Introverted individuals start evacuating earlier than extraverted individuals within groups, contrary to initial hypotheses - extraversion shows moderate positive correlation with delayed evacuation start (rs=0.40, p=0.001)
Pedestrian crowds exhibit clustered density patterns (density spots) rather than homogeneous distribution, with these clusters increasing with group amount, mobility, and group size
Neurotic individuals tend to walk more directly (lower detour index) than emotionally stable individuals, particularly for longer walking distances (interaction effect F=6.935, p=0.01)

limitations.text: Limited sample size for larger groups (5 members: 12 occurrences, 6 members: 9, 7 members: 6, 10 members: 6), affecting statistical validity for groups >4
Only 46% of sample had complete personality and movement data (88 out of 188 matched), potentially introducing selection bias
Study focused exclusively on student population (mean age 21.8 years, SD=1.96, range 19-31), limiting generalizability to other populations
Group detection algorithm unreliable in high-density congestion scenarios where aggregations consist of multiple groups not easily distinguishable by automated methods

future_work: Continue data analysis with larger samples to validate findings about group behavior
Develop pedestrian movement models incorporating group effects and psychological factors
Extend research to complete samples of individuals under critical circumstances
Investigate mechanisms beyond observed correlations between personality and movement behavior
Replicate findings with more diverse population samples
profiles.notes: The paper presents first findings from an interdisciplinary approach combining traffic sciences and psychology. Key novel contributions include: (1) demonstration of horizontal stripe formation in group movement at low densities; (2) evidence that extraversion does not lead to faster evacuation decisions as hypothesized but rather delays; (3) identification of neuroticism effects on path directness. The research uses EPQ-RK (Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Revised) for psychometric profiling and video tracking for movement analysis.

profiles.title: Untitled
profiles.research_problem: Investigating the causal effect of traffic density on pedestrian waiting time in the presence of Automated Vehicles (AVs), while addressing confounding variables that affect both policy (traffic density) and outcome (pedestrian wait time)
profiles.method_summary: Two model structures are employed: (1) Copula-based joint model of pedestrian stress level and waiting time using Frank copula function to address self-selection; (2) Double/Debiased Machine Learning (DML) model that uses Random Forest estimators as nuisance functions to flexibly adjust for confounders and estimate causal effect through residualization
datasets: Virtual Immersive Reality Experiment (VIRE) dataset - 1,406 pedestrian responses

main_claims.claim_text: DML provides a more reliable causal estimate of traffic density on pedestrian wait time with lower standard error (0.132) and narrower confidence interval [-1.123, -1.107] compared to copula approach
Traffic density has a negative causal effect on pedestrian waiting time (-1.115), meaning lower traffic density leads to shorter pedestrian wait times

Copula approach underestimates the effect of traffic density on pedestrian waiting time due to inability to adjust for confounders, while DML provides unbiased causal estimates by addressing spurious relationships
Pedestrian stress level acts as a confounding variable that influences both perception of traffic density and waiting time decisions

limitations.text: The analysis relies on unconfoundedness assumption - all confounding variables must be observed; unobserved confounders are not addressed
Panel effect of the data is not accounted for despite the dataset being classified as panel data
Copula approach only captures associations not causal effects, potentially leading to incorrect policy recommendations

future_work: Compare DML with Structural Causal Model (SCM) which inherently captures causality
Investigate causal models that account for unobserved confounding variables (endogeneity problem)
Account for panel effects in the data
Compare performance of different machine learning causal models (e.g., Causal Forest, Double Random Forest)
Analyze pedestrian group-crossing behavior and role of conformity in collaborative environments

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The problem is choosing rational regimes of traffic light control for both traffic and pedestrian flows at signalized intersections. Existing traffic light control systems operate with limited time parameters and do not adequately consider pedestrian behavior, leading to increased traffic rule violations and reduced safety at grade-level pedestrian crosswalks.
matrix.method_summary: The study employs experimental field measurements of traffic and pedestrian flow indicators at signalized intersections in Lviv city, combined with simulation modeling using PTV Vissim software. Pedestrian behavior was analyzed based on surveillance camera recordings from the city's traffic management center. The research developed quadratic equations modeling the relationship between pedestrian violations and volume-capacity ratio, and determined maximal vehicle queue lengths under different control regimes.
matrix.datasets: field_measurements_from_8_signalized_intersections_and_pedestrian_crosswalks_in_lviv_city_divided_into_3_types; surveillance_camera_data_from_lviv_city_traffic_management_center; ptv_vissim_simulation_data; matlab_computed_dependencies
matrix.metrics: number_of_pedestrian_violations_persons_per_hour; volume_capacity_ratio; vehicle_queue_length_meters; traffic_flow_delay_seconds_per_vehicle; restrictive_signal_duration_seconds
matrix.claim_text: Intersections should be classified into three types based on location characteristics and traffic/pedestrian flow patterns: Type I near transport junctions, Type II in central zones, and Type III in residential zones, each requiring different traffic light control parameters.
matrix.limitations: The study was conducted only in Lviv city, which may limit generalizability to other cities with different traffic patterns, infrastructure, or cultural contexts. | Lane width was found to have no impact on pedestrian behavior but the study focused only on widths between 3.0-4.0 meters. | The model coefficients for violation prediction had R2 values ranging from 0.70 to 0.89, indicating moderate fit accuracy. | Climate conditions and pedestrian discipline factors were identified as important but not fully incorporated into the developed models.
matrix.notes: The study provides practical recommendations for traffic engineers to optimize signal timing based on intersection location type and volume-capacity ratio. The methodology combining field observations with PTV Vissim simulation offers a replicable approach for other urban settings. Key finding is that longer pedestrian waiting times paradoxically increase violations due to group behavior dynamics.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding pedestrian crossing decision-making at uncontrolled intersections with continuous traffic flow, and developing computational models to characterize pedestrian behavior based on behavioral mechanisms. The study addresses gaps in existing pedestrian behavior models which lack psychological underpinnings, don't adequately model traffic flow scenarios, and fail to capture the time-dynamic nature of crossing decisions.
matrix.method_summary: A deconstructed crossing decision-making process is proposed with three stages: visual collision cue, decision, and crossing initiation. The visual collision cue uses the first temporal derivative of visual angle (˙θ) as the primary decision cue. A binary choice logit model with decision strategies for traffic flow scenarios models gap acceptance. Crossing initiation time is modeled using Shifted Wald (SW) distribution. Two datasets from a CAVE-based immersive pedestrian simulator calibrate and validate the models.
matrix.datasets: Dataset one: 60 participants, 4270 trials, single-lane road with two consecutive vehicles at speeds 25/30/35 mph and time gaps 2/3/4/5 s (CAVE simulator); Dataset two: 60 participants, 1920 trials, continuous traffic flow with varying gap sequences (1-8s) across 4 traffic scenarios (CAVE simulator)
matrix.metrics: R-squared (R²); Root Mean Square Error (RMSE); BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion); K-S test (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test); Log-likelihood
matrix.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing decisions can be characterized as dynamically adapting based on perceived collision risk, where the visual collision cue (˙θ, first temporal derivative of visual angle) effectively captures pedestrian gap acceptance behavior across different traffic scenarios
matrix.limitations: Model only covers scenarios with single-lane, constant-speed traffic and cannot be directly generalized to multi-lane scenarios, yielding vehicles, or complex traffic conditions without further development | Experiments conducted in pedestrian simulator may not fully capture real-world behavior variability - virtual nature of task may affect observed behavior compared to naturalistic settings | Model assumes pedestrians use specific visual cues (˙θ) and perception strategies based on current collision perception theory, but actual pedestrian cognitive processes may differ | Model does not account for external factors such as weather conditions, visibility, pedestrian demographics, or social influences that may affect real-world crossing behavior
matrix.notes: The paper introduces anthropomorphic modeling approach to pedestrian crossing decisions by grounding the model in visual perception theory. The deconstructed three-stage process (perceive-decide-execute) provides framework for understanding pedestrian behavior. The study provides a simulation tool for reproducing pedestrian crossing behavior. Model shows good predictive performance with R²=0.890 for gap acceptance and R²=0.850 for crossing initiation time on Dataset one.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: How to quantitatively evaluate the impact of pedestrian policies (specifically lane-like segregation) on pedestrian behavior before implementation, using microscopic simulation
matrix.method_summary: A microscopic pedestrian simulation model (MPSM) based on physical forces (forward force and two types of repulsive forces) using discrete event simulation with difference equations. The model treats pedestrians as autonomous circular objects with individual properties (initial location, time, velocity, target). Unlike similar physical-based models (social force, magnetic force), this model does not require target time as input and outputs dissipation time. Parameters include calibrated values: max speed 1.2 m/s, pedestrian diameter 70 cm, sight distance 4 m, α=15, β=6, mass=2.
matrix.datasets: calibration_using_real_pedestrian_behavior
matrix.metrics: average_speed; instantaneous_delay; dissipation_time
matrix.claim_text: The microscopic pedestrian simulation model based on physical forces can effectively evaluate pedestrian policies quantitatively before implementation
matrix.limitations: The simulation model is merely a numerical experiment rather than a complete real world simulation | Model lacks validation against real-world pedestrian behavior data; validation is recommended for further study
matrix.notes: Model differs from social force and magnetic force models by not requiring target time as input; Uses discrete event simulation without queuing theory; Case study: pedestrian crossing at Tohoku University; Policy implementation involves marking arrows on left side of zebra cross to guide pedestrians

matrix.method_summary: A microscopic pedestrian simulation model (MPSM) based on physical forces (forward force and two types of repulsive forces) using discrete event simulation with difference equations. Parameters include calibrated values: max speed 1.2 m/s, pedestrian diameter 70 cm, sight distance 4 m, α=15, β=6, mass=2.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigating the impact of smartphone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and evaluating the effectiveness of a smart LED light safety treatment using head-mounted immersive virtual reality
matrix.method_summary: A Virtual Immersive/Interactive Reality Environment (VIRE) with Oculus Rift was developed to simulate an unsignalized road crossing in Montreal. Forty-two participants completed 30 successful crossing trials across three conditions: 1) no distraction, 2) distracted with smartphone maze-solving game, and 3) distracted with smartphone plus smart LED safety measure. Data on surrogate safety measures (PET, TTC), crossing behavior, and distraction attributes were collected and analyzed using Multinomial Logit (MNL) models.
matrix.datasets: 42 volunteers from Montreal and Toronto (59.9% Montreal, 40.5% female, ages 18-45); 30 successful crossing trials per participant (10 per condition); Total of approximately 1260 trials analyzed
matrix.metrics: Post-Encroachment Time (PET); Time-to-Collision (TTC); Crossing duration; Wait time duration; Crossing speed; Initial walking speed; Maximum acceleration; Maximum deceleration; Percentage of time looking at smartphone; Number of head orientations to smartphone; Success/failure/time-out rates
matrix.claim_text: Distracted pedestrians have significantly lower PET measures and higher crossing speeds, resulting in more dangerous crossings compared to non-distracted pedestrians
matrix.limitations: Single road crossing location in Montreal limits generalizability to other urban environments | Study only examined interactions between vehicles and pedestrians; mixed traffic scenarios with cyclists were not included | Eye tracking was not used; head orientation was used as proxy for gaze direction | Only maze-solving puzzle was tested as distraction; other distraction types (texting, calling, listening to music) were not examined | Sample size of 42 participants may limit statistical power for detecting smaller effect sizes | VR environment despite high fidelity may not fully replicate real-world pedestrian behavior due to absence of actual physical risk
matrix.notes: Published in Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour; Accepted June 17, 2018; VIRE system replicates real Montreal urban intersection near Laurier metro station; Smart LED lights change from white to blue when distracted crossing is initiated; Participants were not informed of success/failure during trials; Average walking speed of 1.1 m/s consistent with field studies; 62% of participants had never used VR before

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Current pedestrian behavior models for traffic simulation are limited in utility for intelligent driving applications due to situation-specific implementations, rigid environment definitions, limited pedestrian characteristics, simplistic gap estimation for crossing decisions, and rigid decision-making steps. There is a need for highly configurable simulations that can generate diverse pedestrian populations with realistic crossing behaviors.
matrix.method_summary: The paper proposes Intend-Wait-Cross, an agent-based microscopic pedestrian behavior model that generates realistic crossing decisions. The model incorporates: (1) six individual characteristics: type (adult/child/elderly), trait (aggressive/conservative/average), law obedience, gap acceptance, crossing pattern (one-stage/rolling gap), and perceptual noise; (2) a discrete choice model for multiple crossing scenarios; (3) an improved TTC computation accounting for vehicle relevance (positional and intentional), dynamic vehicle behavior, pedestrian travel time, vehicle length, and perceptual noise. The model is implemented in SUMO traffic simulation platform.
matrix.method_family: Agent-based modeling / Microscopic traffic simulation / Discrete choice model
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing decision-making at designated crosswalks and jaywalking scenarios; Pedestrian trajectory generation; Traffic simulation for autonomous driving applications
matrix.datasets: PIE (Pedestrian Intention Estimation) dataset - naturalistic driving data; Knoblauch et al. walking speed field study; Schmidt et al. gap acceptance study (Europe); Ashworth gap acceptance study (Europe); Brewer et al. gap acceptance study (North America)
matrix.metrics: Wait time; Minimum TTC at time of crossing; Number of vehicle-pedestrian collisions; Crossing duration; % of completed illegal crossings
matrix.claim_text: The Intend-Wait-Cross model generates diverse pedestrian behaviors by incorporating individual characteristics (type, trait, law obedience, gap acceptance, crossing pattern, perceptual noise) that are motivated by traffic behavior research.
matrix.limitations: The vehicle reactive behavior model has limitations - vehicles only detect pedestrians when paths are aligned, causing remaining accidents when pedestrians cross very close to intersections and vehicles are about to turn | The model parameters for trait, law-obedience, and crosswalk distance threshold were set empirically rather than derived from naturalistic data due to lack of available data | Pedestrian gap acceptance values were set based on European studies which may not generalize to all countries and environments
matrix.notes: Published on arXiv (2203.07324v1) in March 2022. Implementation uses SUMO (v1.9.0) traffic simulation platform. Model is motivated by traffic behavior literature and calibrated using naturalistic driving data (PIE dataset) and estimates from the literature. The paper addresses the need for realistic pedestrian simulations for training and evaluating autonomous driving systems.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the lack of pedestrian experience consideration in intelligent transportation systems (ITS). Traffic signals traditionally focus on vehicle throughput and waiting time while overlooking pedestrians. There is no accurate simulation framework for estimating pedestrian crossing time at signalized intersections, which is needed for dynamic traffic light scheduling that balances pedestrian safety and convenience with vehicular flow.
matrix.method_summary: The paper presents a novel Pedestrian Road Crossing Simulator (PCS) that models pedestrian motion at signalized intersections at a fine-grained level. PCS uses a geometric approach where each pedestrian is represented by a circle representing occupied space that cannot overlap with others. The simulator employs Normal distribution for pedestrian initial positioning in waiting areas, models different pedestrian types (healthy adults, elders, children, disabled persons) with varying speeds, and uses exclusion arc calculations to determine optimal movement paths. The system is validated using field data collected from two intersections in Xi'an, China, and integrated with SUMO traffic simulation for TLS optimization experiments.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing time estimation at signalized intersections; Dynamic traffic light scheduling optimization incorporating pedestrian needs; Pedestrian behavior simulation including group dynamics and mobility constraints
matrix.datasets: Field data from intersection of Ming Guang Road and Feng Cheng No. 9 Road, Xi'an (Location1) - crosswalk dimensions 47.69m x 6.4m (North/South), 43.62m x 3.6m (West/East); Field data from intersection of Wei Yang Road and Feng Cheng No. 7 Road, Xi'an (Location2) - crosswalk dimensions 45.045m x 3.6m; Pedestrian walking speed distribution: N(1.2676, 0.09167); Video recordings from mobile phones documenting pedestrian and vehicle counts, directions, and crossing times; Synthetic traffic data: 990 vehicles and 522 pedestrians
matrix.metrics: Estimation accuracy of crossing time (98.37% claimed); Vehicle average waiting time; Pedestrian average waiting time; Vehicle throughput; Maximum waiting time
matrix.claim_text: PCS achieves 98.37% accuracy in estimating pedestrian crossing time compared to real field measurements
matrix.notes: PCS is made available as open source at github.com/DayuanTan/OpenSourceAcademicResearch; The simulator uses SUMO v1.9.2 for traffic simulation and Python 3.7.2 for implementation; Field study collected data at two different times (2pm and 7pm) to capture different traffic densities; Three distributions tested for pedestrian positioning: Normal, Poisson, and T distributions; Buffer zones around crosswalks were introduced based on field observations of actual pedestrian behavior

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses the lack of pedestrian experience consideration in intelligent transportation systems (ITS). Traffic signals traditionally focus on vehicle throughput and waiting time while overlooking pedestrians. There is no accurate simulation framework for estimating pedestrian crossing time at signalized intersections, which is needed for dynamic traffic light scheduling that balances pedestrian safety and convenience with vehicular flow.
matrix.method_summary: The paper presents a novel Pedestrian Road Crossing Simulator (PCS) that models pedestrian motion at signalized intersections at a fine-grained level. PCS uses a geometric approach where each pedestrian is represented by a circle representing occupied space that cannot overlap with others. The simulator employs Normal distribution for pedestrian initial positioning in waiting areas, models different pedestrian types (healthy adults, elders, children, disabled persons) with varying speeds, and uses exclusion arc calculations to determine optimal movement paths. The system is validated using field data collected from two intersections in Xi'an, China, and integrated with SUMO traffic simulation for TLS optimization experiments.
matrix.tasks: Pedestrian crossing time estimation at signalized intersections; Dynamic traffic light scheduling optimization incorporating pedestrian needs; Pedestrian behavior simulation including group dynamics and mobility constraints
matrix.datasets: Field data from intersection of Ming Guang Road and Feng Cheng No. 9 Road, Xi'an (Location1) - crosswalk dimensions 47.69m x 6.4m (North/South), 43.62m x 3.6m (West/East); Field data from intersection of Wei Yang Road and Feng Cheng No. 7 Road, Xi'an (Location2) - crosswalk dimensions 45.045m x 3.6m; Pedestrian walking speed distribution: N(1.2676, 0.09167); Video recordings from mobile phones documenting pedestrian and vehicle counts, directions, and crossing times; Synthetic traffic data: 990 vehicles and 522 pedestrians
matrix.metrics: Estimation accuracy of crossing time (98.37% claimed); Vehicle average waiting time; Pedestrian average waiting time; Vehicle throughput; Maximum waiting time
matrix.claim_text: Integrating PCS into dynamic TLS reduces pedestrian average waiting time by 77.44% compared to static TLS while maintaining high vehicular throughput
matrix.notes: PCS is made available as open source at github.com/DayuanTan/OpenSourceAcademicResearch; The simulator uses SUMO v1.9.2 for traffic simulation and Python 3.7.2 for implementation; Field study collected data at two different times (2pm and 7pm) to capture different traffic densities; Three distributions tested for pedestrian positioning: Normal, Poisson, and T distributions; Buffer zones around crosswalks were introduced based on field observations of actual pedestrian behavior

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Understanding collective traffic phenomena of molecular motors in cells, including traffic jam formation, boundary-induced phase transitions, and spontaneous symmetry breaking in multi-species motor systems
matrix.method_summary: Uses driven lattice gas models (variants of asymmetric exclusion processes) to describe molecular motor traffic. Models incorporate active movement of bound motors as biased random walks, passive diffusion of unbound motors, motor-filament binding/unbinding dynamics, and motor-motor mutual exclusion interactions. Three-dimensional cubic lattice with filament sites and non-filament sites, with rates for stepping, unbinding, rebinding, and exclusion.
matrix.metrics: bound_motor_density_rhob; unbound_motor_density_rhoub; motor_current_J; traffic_jam_length_L_star; phase_diagrams
matrix.claim_text: For filaments in contact with a constant motor reservoir (large unbound motor pool), the traffic jam length is of the order of the motor walking distance Δxb = vb/ε0
matrix.limitations: Analysis assumes unbound motor density gradients along the filament direction can be neglected, which may not hold in all experimental situations | Theoretical predictions have limited experimental validation; only a few observations of motor traffic jams exist in vivo and in vitro | Model parameters (binding/unbinding rates, diffusion coefficients) must be carefully matched to specific motor types (kinesin, dynein, myosin) for quantitative predictions
matrix.notes: This paper provides a theoretical framework connecting molecular motor biology with statistical physics of driven diffusive systems. Key insight: unlike macroscopic traffic, molecular motors have finite run lengths and can unbind/rebind, leading to unique collective phenomena. The work bridges biophysical experiments with nonequilibrium statistical mechanics.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Developing a stochastic cellular automaton model for pedestrian dynamics that can quantitatively reproduce the fundamental diagram and self-organizing behaviors while compensating for deficiencies caused by the discrete grid structure
matrix.method_summary: A microscopic stochastic cellular automaton model using a 3x3 transition matrix (Moore neighborhood) for agent movements. The model includes grid-based path planning via flood fill algorithm with Moore neighborhood distance metric, compensation strategies for grid-induced directional dependencies, and a dynamic motion field for mid-range agent interactions that stores both past positions and extrapolated future positions. The model uses shuffled sequential update procedure and motion traces.
matrix.datasets: infinite_environment_simulation
matrix.metrics: speed_density_relation; fundamental_diagram; distance_error; lane_formation_emergence
matrix.claim_text: The stochastic cellular automaton model successfully reproduces the characteristic shape of the fundamental diagram of pedestrian dynamics with vmax = 4 (3) speed characteristic
matrix.limitations: The regular grid structure causes inherent directional dependencies affecting motion speed and variance, particularly at diagonal transitions | The stochastic cell selection process introduces additional motion variance for non-axis transition angles | Lane formations are not stable in time and space due to the inherent stochastic nature of the model | The Moore metric tends to overestimate distances compared to Euclidean metric
matrix.notes: Model uses 3-4 motion steps per simulation turn depending on agent density; The dynamic motion field combines past positions and future positions with isotropic spatial diffusion and temporal decay; The algorithm does not require computation-time expensive sorting or square root calculations; Counter-flow scenario demonstrates separation of agents and lane formation

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigating the impact of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems on traffic dynamics, specifically whether automated driving strategies can improve road capacity and traffic stability or will necessarily increase heterogeneity and instability.
matrix.method_summary: Microscopic traffic simulations using the Intelligent Driver Model (IDM) to study mixed traffic consisting of human-driven and ACC-equipped vehicles on a single-lane road with an on-ramp bottleneck. Three ACC parameter sets were tested varying time headway (λT), acceleration (λa), and deceleration (λb).
matrix.datasets: synthetic_simulation_data_generated_from_idm
matrix.metrics: maximum_free_capacity_Q_free_max; dynamic_capacity_Q_out; actual_travel_time; cumulated_travel_time; flow_density_relationship; traffic_breakdown_threshold
matrix.claim_text: A small proportion of ACC vehicles (as low as 10%) leads to a drastic reduction of traffic congestion, with maximum travel time delay reduced by about 30% and cumulated time delay reduced by 50%
matrix.limitations: Single-lane scenario does not account for multi-lane effects that are relevant in real traffic | Trucks are excluded from simulations, which may affect real-world applicability | Merging, lane changing and gap-creation behaviors are not modeled (still require human driver intervention) | Results are based on synthetic simulation data rather than empirical validation with real ACC-equipped vehicles
matrix.notes: Paper published in 2006 on arXiv (physics.soc-ph). Uses Intelligent Driver Model (IDM) for both human and ACC driving, differentiating only by parameter sets. Key finding is that small percentages of ACC can have disproportionate positive effects on traffic flow due to capacity improvements and stabilization.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Predicting pedestrian crossing behavior including crossing decision (cross or wait), crossing initiation time (CIT), and crossing duration (CD) when interacting with vehicles at unsignalized crossings
matrix.method_summary: Machine learning models (logistic regression, SVM, random forest, neural network MLP) to predict pedestrian crossing behavior using features including time to arrival (TTA), waiting time, crossing location type, age, gender, SVO (social value orientation), and AISS (Arnett inventory of sensation seeking). Uses distributed simulator data with 64 participant pairs and 1279 trials.
matrix.method_family: Machine learning - Logistic Regression, Support-Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) neural network
matrix.tasks: Crossing decision prediction (cross or wait - binary classification); Crossing initiation time (CIT) prediction (regression); Crossing duration (CD) prediction (regression)
matrix.datasets: Distributed Simulator Study (DSS) - University of Leeds Driving Simulator (UoLDS) connected to HIKER pedestrian lab; 64 participant pairs (32 drivers, 32 pedestrians); 1279 total trials, 836 crossing cases; Five TTA conditions (3, 4, 5, 6, 7 seconds); Four crossing locations (two zebra, two non-zebra)
matrix.metrics: Accuracy (ACC) and F1 score for classification; Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for regression
matrix.claim_text: The proposed MLP model improves crossing decision prediction accuracy by 4.46% and F1 score by 3.23% compared to logistic regression baseline
matrix.limitations: The study uses simulator data which may not fully represent real-world pedestrian behavior | In practice, it is difficult to obtain all features (personality traits, age, gender) used in the models, limiting real-world applicability | Performance degrades significantly when using fewer input features, particularly for non-linear models | Smaller TTA values lead to lower prediction accuracy due to increased uncertainty in pedestrian behavior
matrix.notes: Paper was accepted for publication in 2023 IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV). Funded by European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (SHAPE-IT project, grant agreement 860410). The study is novel in considering both objective factors and subjective personality traits (SVO, AISS) for pedestrian-vehicle interaction prediction at unsignalized crossings.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: The paper addresses limitations in current pedestrian dynamics models, which primarily focus on operational movement aspects (spatial exclusion, distance and direction repulsion) while neglecting psychological influences and group coordination. The research investigates group dynamic behavior and psychometric profiles as drivers for pedestrian dynamics, aiming to incorporate psychophysical concepts and intra-group coordination into movement models.
matrix.method_summary: The study uses mixed methods: (1) Field observations at German Protestant Kirchentag mass event (120,000 participants) to analyze group behavior and density patterns; (2) Evacuation experiment at TU Dresden with 301 students, combining video tracking and psychometric questionnaires (EPQ-RK); (3) Statistical analysis including t-tests, ANOVAs, and Spearman correlations to examine personality effects on movement decisions and walking patterns.
matrix.method_family: Empirical observational study / Mixed methods (quantitative movement tracking + psychometric questionnaires)
matrix.tasks: Group detection and tracking from video data; Movement trajectory analysis; Personality trait measurement (extraversion, neuroticism, psychoticism via EPQ-RK); Analysis of pre-movement phase parameters (tready, tstart); Analysis of movement phase parameters (walking speed, speed deviation, detour index); Density-speed relationship analysis (fundamental diagram)
matrix.datasets: German Protestant Kirchentag at Dresden (June 1-5, 2011) - approximately 120,000 fulltime participants and 50,000 guests; Evacuation experiment at TU Dresden - 301 students participated, 259 completed questionnaires, 188 matched with movement data, 88 with complete personality data
matrix.metrics: Fundamental diagram (density-speed-relation); Five-number summary descriptive statistics (boxplot); Probability density function (PDF) for relative positions; Z-standardized time deviations from group mean; Detour index; Speed deviation
matrix.claim_text: Group size significantly affects pedestrian speed characteristics - median and standard deviation decrease with group size up to 5 members, then show an unexpected increase at larger groups
matrix.limitations: Limited sample size for larger groups (5 members: 12 occurrences, 6 members: 9, 7 members: 6, 10 members: 6), affecting statistical validity for groups >4 | Only 46% of sample had complete personality and movement data (88 out of 188 matched), potentially introducing selection bias | Study focused exclusively on student population (mean age 21.8 years, SD=1.96, range 19-31), limiting generalizability to other populations | Group detection algorithm unreliable in high-density congestion scenarios where aggregations consist of multiple groups not easily distinguishable by automated methods
matrix.notes: The paper presents first findings from an interdisciplinary approach combining traffic sciences and psychology. Key novel contributions include: (1) demonstration of horizontal stripe formation in group movement at low densities; (2) evidence that extraversion does not lead to faster evacuation decisions as hypothesized but rather delays; (3) identification of neuroticism effects on path directness. The research uses EPQ-RK (Eysenck Personality Questionnaire Revised) for psychometric profiling and video tracking for movement analysis.

matrix.title: Untitled
matrix.research_problem: Investigating the causal effect of traffic density on pedestrian waiting time in the presence of Automated Vehicles (AVs), while addressing confounding variables that affect both policy (traffic density) and outcome (pedestrian wait time)
matrix.method_summary: Two model structures are employed: (1) Copula-based joint model of pedestrian stress level and waiting time using Frank copula function to address self-selection; (2) Double/Debiased Machine Learning (DML) model that uses Random Forest estimators as nuisance functions to flexibly adjust for confounders and estimate causal effect through residualization
matrix.datasets: Virtual Immersive Reality Experiment (VIRE) dataset - 1,406 pedestrian responses
matrix.metrics: BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion); standard_error; confidence_interval; cost_value
matrix.claim_text: DML provides a more reliable causal estimate of traffic density on pedestrian wait time with lower standard error (0.132) and narrower confidence interval [-1.123, -1.107] compared to copula approach
matrix.limitations: The analysis relies on unconfoundedness assumption - all confounding variables must be observed; unobserved confounders are not addressed | Panel effect of the data is not accounted for despite the dataset being classified as panel data | Copula approach only captures associations not causal effects, potentially leading to incorrect policy recommendations

investigation_of_traffic_and_pedestrian_.claim_text: Intersections should be classified into three types based on location characteristics and traffic/pedestrian flow patterns: Type I near transport junctions, Type II in central zones, and Type III in residential zones, each requiring different traffic light control parameters.

The smallest number of pedestrian violations occurs with restrictive signal duration of 40-60 seconds at volume-capacity ratios of 0.50-0.65 for Type I, 0.40-0.65 for Type II, and 0.45-0.55 for Type III intersections.

The maximum vehicle queue length varies significantly by intersection type, reaching 504.56 m for Type I at volume-capacity ratio 0.88, compared to 353.65 m for Type II and 257.88 m for Type III under similar conditions.

Rational traffic light control should limit restrictive signal duration to maximum 50 seconds for Type I, 90 seconds for Type II, and 110 seconds for Type III intersections, regardless of control phase number.

gap_acceptance_prediction.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing decisions can be characterized as dynamically adapting based on perceived collision risk, where the visual collision cue (˙θ, first temporal derivative of visual angle) effectively captures pedestrian gap acceptance behavior across different traffic scenarios

The SW-PRD model (Shifted Wald-based Pedestrian Road-crossing Decision model) significantly outperforms the G-PRD model on both datasets, achieving better BIC values, higher log-likelihoods, and passing K-S tests while G-PRD fails

The proposed decision strategy for continuous traffic flow (comparing collision cues of current gap with maximum rejected gap and following gap) significantly improves model performance in traffic flow scenarios

Pedestrians dynamically adjust their crossing initiation time based on vehicle kinematics - initiating faster when facing higher collision threat - demonstrating the time-dynamic nature of crossing decisions

The model reproduces pedestrian crossing behavior patterns in continuous traffic flow, including reduced tendency to accept gaps when upcoming larger gaps exist and greater tendency to reject gaps after previously rejecting similar or larger gaps

Pedestrian crossing decision-making at d.claim_text: The Intend-Wait-Cross model generates diverse pedestrian behaviors by incorporating individual characteristics (type, trait, law obedience, gap acceptance, crossing pattern, perceptual noise) that are motivated by traffic behavior research.

The dynamic TTC computation with adjustment for pedestrian travel time and vehicle length (Dynamic+adj) nearly eliminates vehicle-pedestrian collisions (0.0-0.4 collisions) across light, medium, and heavy traffic conditions, compared to 3.2-7.0 collisions with constant and average methods.
